title: Germany Seeks US Missile Production Deal Ahead of Ankara Summit as European Defense Industrial Base Scrambles
subhead: Berlin pushes to produce Patriot and Tomahawk missiles on German soil days before the NATO summit in Ankara, while Saab finalizes a $2.54B Gripen deal with Ukraine, Dassault eyes new partners after FCAS collapse, and Russian ships arm themselves in the Baltic.

sections.heading: Germany Courts Washington for Domestic Patriot and Tomahawk Production
sections.body:
Berlin is in discussions with Washington to manufacture PAC-3 Patriot missiles and possibly Tomahawk cruise missiles on German soil, the Financial Times reported, citing people familiar with the matter. The talks form part of a wider push to stabilize ties with the Trump administration and advance defense deals during the NATO summit in Ankara on July 7-8.

One source described the deal as focused on 'joint production concepts,' while another said it covers 'anything' that could bolster bilateral defense — including PAC-3 and Tomahawk. A third source told the FT that the US response has been more positive than initially expected. German officials reportedly argued the deal could help restore Washington's own depleted Patriot stockpile — drained by the Iran campaign — while simultaneously helping Europe rearm.

Currently, PAC-3 missiles are only produced in the US and under license in Japan. Germany produces the older PAC-2 variants domestically, some destined for Ukraine under a EUR 4 billion ($4.7 billion) deal signed in April. For the Tomahawk, Berlin has engaged European arms maker MBDA to work with Raytheon on a ground-launched variant, though MBDA has not yet entered formal talks. The Tomahawk has been primarily a naval weapon; only a handful of ground-based launchers exist in various stages of development.

Rheinmetall led German defense stocks higher on the FT report. The company stands to benefit as a key industrial partner in any German-based production arrangement.
sections.whyItMatters: If finalized, this would be the first licensed Tomahawk production outside the US and only the second Patriot production line globally — a significant transfer of defense industrial capacity to Europe at a moment when US stockpiles are strained and European demand for air defense is acute.

sections.heading: Saab Signs $2.54 Billion Gripen E Contract With Ukraine
sections.body:
Swedish defense manufacturer Saab signed a contract on June 30 to deliver 16 Gripen E fighter aircraft to Ukraine, valued at approximately 24.6 billion Swedish crowns ($2.54 billion). The deal, finalized following an agreement between Swedish Prime Minister Ulf Kristersson and President Volodymyr Zelenskyy, represents Kyiv's first firm combat jet procurement from a Western manufacturer.

There is a discrepancy in delivery timelines: Zelenskyy stated deliveries would begin in 2027, while Saab's contract specifies 2029-2030. Breaking Defense reported that the deal is a first step in Kyiv's ambition to purchase up to 150 of the advanced combat jets, per Swedish Defense Minister Pal Jonson.

The Gripen E is designed to operate from short stretches, temporary runways, and roads — features well-suited to Ukraine's dispersed-operations doctrine. However, the delivery gap means Ukraine will rely on existing air defense and donated F-16s for years before Gripens arrive in theater.
sections.whyItMatters: Ukraine now has a binding contract for next-generation Western combat aircraft, but the 2-4 year delivery window highlights the persistent gap between procurement announcements and battlefield availability. The 150-aircraft aspiration, if funded, would reshape the air balance over the long term.

sections.heading: FCAS Collapse Sends Dassault Shopping for New Partners
sections.body:
Dassault Aviation CEO Eric Trappier said the company is 'completely open to cooperation' on a future fighter program following the collapse of the Franco-German-Spanish Future Combat Air System (FCAS), Reuters and Aviation Week reported on July 1. Trappier left open the prospect of working with a non-European partner — a notable shift for the French defense champion that has long guarded its independence in combat jet design.

Germany and Spain have already launched their own successor program, dubbed 'Team Gen 6,' after the FCAS partnership fell apart over industrial workshare disputes. The split leaves Europe with no unified sixth-generation fighter program, at a time when the US, UK, and Japan each have their own next-gen efforts underway.

The FCAS collapse is the latest in a long line of failed European joint fighter projects, from the Eurofighter to the Dassault-BAE BAE Demo. The pattern of national industrial interests overriding collaboration continues to fragment Europe's defense aerospace sector.
sections.whyItMatters: Europe's inability to sustain a joint sixth-generation fighter program means it will develop competing platforms, raise per-unit costs, and risk falling behind US and Chinese timelines. Dassault's openness to a non-European partner could reshape alliance dynamics in fighter procurement.

sections.heading: Baltic Escalation: Russia Arms Civilian LNG Carrier; Shadow Fleet Seizures Mount
sections.body:
Estonia released surveillance images showing machine gun positions and sandbags mounted on the Marshal Vasilevskiy, a Russian-flagged LNG carrier, while sailing in the Gulf of Finland this spring. The ship, owned by Gazprom Flot LLC and home-ported in Kaliningrad, sails a route between Kaliningrad and St Petersburg that runs along Estonia's coastline.

Analyst Yoruk Isik of the Bosphorus Observer consultancy called it a 'hostile message to EU and NATO nations that it will actively oppose any attempt to detain or inspect its ships,' noting there is 'no justification for self-defense posture like a machine gun in the Baltic.' A Danish Navy commander assessed that NATO vessels approaching the Marshal Vasilevskiy would likely be fired upon. Approximately half the vessel's crew have backgrounds in the Russian military, National Guard, or FSB, The Baltic Flank reported.

The development comes as shadow fleet seizures accelerate: nine suspected shadow fleet oil tankers have been seized across Europe since the start of 2026, most recently by France last week. The UK announced new sanctions on the Russian shadow fleet at the G7, and Canada imposed sanctions on 162 entities targeting Russian energy revenues. Ukrainian officials argued that shadow fleet vessels should be treated as military targets under international law.

Estonian Navy Commander Ivo Vark said Estonia has stopped attempting to detain shadow fleet vessels that do not pose immediate danger, citing escalation risks — a sign of how seriously the Baltic states take the potential for armed confrontation at sea.
sections.whyItMatters: Russia is militarizing civilian shipping in the Baltic and daring NATO to intercept. With Estonia halting seizures over escalation fears and armed Russian vessels transiting NATO territorial approaches, the Baltic is becoming a maritime flashpoint that existing NATO rules of engagement may not adequately address.

sections.heading: NATO Ankara Summit: 5% Target Under Strain as Europeans Prepare to Fill US Gaps
sections.body:
When 32 NATO leaders convene in Ankara on July 7-8, the alliance faces a triple challenge: defense spending targets that key members are nowhere near meeting, a US drawdown from Europe, and the need to present a united front on Ukraine.

NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte told an Atlantic Council event on June 25 that he aims to establish a 'credible path' toward the 5% of GDP defense spending target by 2035, agreed at last year's The Hague summit. But Spain still hasn't committed to the target, and POLITICO reported that Czechia, Hungary, Slovenia, and the UK have made little progress since 2025. Only 23 of 32 members had reached the previous 2% threshold by last year.

Reuters reported that NATO is set to announce at Ankara that European members will fill almost all gaps left by the departing US military in the alliance's defense plans — a significant shift in burden-sharing. Turkish Defence Minister Yasar Guler told Reuters that Washington has 'no intention of withdrawing' from NATO but wants Europeans and Canadians to assume more responsibility. Guler also pushed for Turkey's inclusion in European defense initiatives, arguing that excluding NATO's second-largest army 'is a strategically inaccurate approach.'

Rutte said allies will announce tens of billions of dollars in defense-related deals at the summit. The German Patriot/Tomahawk production discussions and the Saab Gripen contract are likely part of that slate.
sections.whyItMatters: The Ankara summit will test whether NATO can translate a spending pledge into actual capability — and whether European nations can credibly backfill US force reductions. Turkey's demand for inclusion in European defense frameworks adds another layer of complexity to alliance burden-sharing.

sections.heading: Hormuz Demining in Limbo as Germany Waits on Iran Deal Terms
sections.body:
Germany's commitment to a Strait of Hormuz mine-clearing mission remains in diplomatic limbo. Foreign Minister Johann Wadephul reaffirmed Berlin's readiness for the operation on July 2, telling reporters in Buenos Aires: 'Our readiness continues to be there.' But he said preconditions for deployment must be met, referring to ongoing US-Iran negotiations in Doha.

Hours earlier, Defence Minister Boris Pistorius struck a different tone in Berlin, indicating he could recall two German naval vessels — the minesweeper Fulda and supply ship Mosel, dispatched to Djibouti in mid-June — saying 'they will not wait forever.' Pistorius said he could not see the preconditions for the operation being met 'in the foreseeable future.'

France and Britain are pushing a multinational Hormuz naval mission as the Iran ceasefire framework takes hold, but the timeline for full shipping restoration remains uncertain. Analysts estimate 40-50 days of mine-clearing operations will be needed before commercial traffic can return to normal levels. Oil spot premiums have already fallen to pre-war levels.
sections.whyItMatters: The gap between Wadephul's diplomatic commitment and Pistorius's operational patience illustrates the tension between alliance solidarity and practical military constraints. If Germany pulls its minesweepers before the mission begins, the Hormuz clearance effort loses a key asset.

sections.heading: Defense Startups Repurpose Auto and Fracking Supply Chains for Weapons Production
sections.body:
Defense tech startups are repurposing automotive chips, fracking pipes, and pharmaceutical production methods to speed Pentagon deliveries, Reuters reported on July 1. The approach sidesteps the Pentagon's traditional procurement bottlenecks by sourcing components from commercial supply chains that already operate at scale.

Automotive-grade chips are being qualified for weapons guidance systems. Fracking-grade steel pipes are being adapted for rocket motor casings. Pharma-style continuous manufacturing processes are being applied to munitions filling and assembly. Ursa Major, a startup, is 3D-printing solid rocket motors for hypersonic weapons, cutting production timelines from months to weeks.

The shift reflects a pragmatic recognition that defense-specific supply chains cannot scale fast enough to meet demand created by the Iran campaign, Ukraine, and European rearmament simultaneously. But it also raises questions about qualification standards, reliability under combat conditions, and the long-term viability of relying on commercial-grade components in military systems.
sections.whyItMatters: The Pentagon's procurement bottleneck is real, and startups are finding creative workarounds — but swapping automotive chips into missiles is not a long-term industrial strategy. The trend reveals both the urgency of the production crisis and the gap between quick fixes and sustainable defense manufacturing.

bottomLine: A week before the Ankara NATO summit, the defense industry story is about who builds what and where. Germany wants to produce American missiles on its soil, Ukraine locked in a $2.54B fighter deal with deliveries years away, Dassault is shopping for new partners after FCAS collapsed, and startups are bolting car chips into weapons because traditional supply chains cannot keep up. Meanwhile, Russia is mounting machine guns on civilian gas carriers in the Baltic, and Germany cannot decide whether to keep its minesweepers near Hormuz. The alliance's industrial and diplomatic threads are pulling in different directions — and the summit will show whether they can be woven into something coherent.
sources: Financial Times, Kyiv Post, Reuters, Forbes, Breaking Defense, Aviation Week, Guardian, ERR News (Estonia), The Baltic Flank, Yahoo News, Seeking Alpha, MarketWatch, SCMP, US News & World Report, South China Morning Post, AOL/Mirror
